elements.sent-2411-001: The goal of this paper is to define a notion of non-commutative Gelfand duality.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using techniques from derived algebraic geometry, we show that category of rings is anti-equivalent to a subcategory of pre-ringed spaces, inspired by Grothendieck's work on commutative rings.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our notion of spectrum, although formally reminiscent of the Grothendieck spectrum, is new; in particular, for commutative rings it does not always agree with the Grothendieck spectrum, but it always projects onto it.
elements.sent-2411-004: Remarkably, an appropriately defined relative version of our spectrum agrees with the Grothendieck spectrum for finitely generated commutative $\mathbb{C}$-algebras.
elements.sent-2411-005: This work aims to represent the starting point for a rigorous study of geometric properties of quantum spacetimes.
elements.sent-2411-007: The notion of duality lies at the heart of many of the paradigm shifts that took place in mathematics and physics in the last couple of centuries.
elements.sent-2411-008: On the one hand, switching perspective brings a breath of fresh air and new insight in situations where progress seemed precluded.
elements.sent-2411-009: On the other hand, changing point of view reveals new aspects that remained otherwise hidden.
elements.sent-2411-010: In physics this is the case, for example, for Einstein's theory of General Relativity: describing the gravitational interactions in purely geometric terms unveiled striking and unexpected physical phenomena, such as black holes, gravitational deflection of light, and gravitational waves, which could not be seen through the prism of Newton's classical theory of gravitation.
elements.sent-2411-011: In mathematics, to name but one example, we can mention Grothendieck's theory of schemes, which has been used to define \'etale cohomology leading to the solution of the Riemann hypothesis for varieties over finite fields.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this paper, we are interested in the duality between algebraic structures and geometric spaces.
elements.sent-2411-013: One of the major contributions in this area is due to Gelfand.
elements.sent-2411-014: In his seminal paper~\cite{gelfand , he showed that a compact Hausdorff topological space can be functorially reconstructed from its Banach algebra of continuous functions.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Conversely, the Gelfand spectrum of the commutative $C^*$-algebra of continuous functions is homeomorphic to the underlying compact Hausdorff topological space.
elements.sent-2411-016: Loosely speaking, Gelfand promoted the idea that any abstract commutative complex Banach algebra can be represented as the ring of functions on a topological space whose points are in correspondence with the maximal ideals of the algebra.
elements.sent-2411-017: It is no overstatement to say that this deep intuition had a transformative effect across all areas of pure mathematics, from functional analysis to --- crucially --- modern algebraic geometry, and continues to inform and inspire cutting-edge research to this day.
elements.paragraph-2411-003: Algebraic geometry and the spectrum of a commutative ring.
elements.sent-2411-018: The branch of mathematics where Gelfand's ideas have had the strongest impact is algebraic geometry.
elements.sent-2411-019: The modern foundations of algebraic geometry were laid in the second half of the twentieth century by the French school led by Grothendieck, based on a variant of Gelfand duality for the category of commutative rings.
elements.sent-2411-020: In this case, to a commutative ring one associates a topological space --- called spectrum --- whose points correspond to the prime ideals of the ring, and whose topology is defined in the style of the topology defined by Zariski in his study of algebraic varieties over $\C$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Elements of the ring are interpreted as functions over the spectrum, so that a perfect duality between spaces and commutative rings is obtained via the theory of locally ringed spaces, briefly recalled in Section~\ref{sec:gelfand .}
elements.sent-2411-022: In this work, we focus on generalising to noncommutative rings the duality between commutative rings and affine schemes pertinent to algebraic geometry.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, the essential ideas behind of our methods apply to the $C^*$-algebra setting as well, or even to more general situations.
elements.sent-2411-024: To obtain the latter (analytic) versions of our noncommutative Gelfand duality, one is required to work in a different framework, one where the methods from homological algebra can be applied effectively to the theory of Banach spaces, or more general functional-analytic settings.
elements.sent-2411-025: This is the case, for example, for the bornological HAG context developed in the series of papers \cite{bambi3,bambi4,bambi2,bambi,BamMi,BamMi2 (see also the recent \cite{BKK for a more comprehensive treatment of the subject).}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Alternatively, one could also work in the framework of condensed mathematics set out in \cite{Scholze .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In the present work, for the sake of clarity, we avoid these further complications and stick with the algebraic setting where the methods of homological and homotopical algebra are applied in the classical setting of the categories of modules over a ring.
elements.sent-2411-028: Derived categories and homological algebra are the key tools we will use to move from the commutative to the noncommutative setting.
elements.paragraph-2411-004: Motivation and heuristics for noncommutative dualities.
elements.sent-2411-029: Despite being of interest in its own right, an important motivation for seeking a noncommutive analogue of the Gelfand duality originates from mathematical physics, in that such a duality could lead to the rigorous formulation of a quantum theory of gravitation.
elements.sent-2411-030: Indeed, although a full quantisation scheme for Einstein's equation is still beyond reach, it is clear from a physical perspective that spacetime should eventually become ``noncommutative''~\cite{Froeb .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The general idea is to construct a noncommutative algebra which possesses a ring epimorphism to the ring of smooth functions over a manifolds, the latter being the ring of functions of the spacetime at hand via the smooth version of the Gelfand duality~\cite{smoothGelfand .}
elements.sent-2411-032: This procedure is conceptually analogous to the quantisation of the classical phase space in the quantum-mechanical setting.
elements.sent-2411-033: This physical principle has been realised in a variety of concrete approaches to the problem, including Connes's noncommutative spectral triples~\cite{connes1,connes2 , Lorentzian spectral triples~\cite{Franco,verch , and deformation quantisation~\cite{Buchholz,waldmann,Grosse .}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: A very popular strategy is to promote Cartesian coordinates describing the flat Minkowski spacetime to operators, and postulate commutation relations in terms of a constant skew-symmetric matrix whose entries are of the order of the Planck length, resulting in the so-called DFR spacetime~\cite{DFR .}
elements.sent-2411-035: The spacetime is then interpreted as a noncommutative Frechét algebra which becomes commutative in the classical limit.
elements.sent-2411-036: The main shortcoming in all these approaches is that the definition of ``noncommutative space'' is realised only at a formal level, so that the geometric properties of such noncommutative space are difficult to capture; indeed, the existing strategies do not even lead to a satisfactory definition of what a point of a noncommutative space would be.
elements.sent-2411-037: Remarkably, there has been evidence that noncommutative geometry manifests itself ``in real life"; for instance, aperiodic crystals~\cite{Bellissard1,Bellissard2 and dirty superconductors~\cite{DeNittis are mathematically modelled by noncommutative tori.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Therefore, it would be rather interesting to study the spectrum of a noncommutative torus or more generally of a symplectic twisted group algebras, see e.g.~\cite{NCtorus,NCtori .. \color{black.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In the literature, there have been many attempts aimed at extending the Gelfand spectrum to the noncommutive $C^*$-algebra.
elements.sent-2411-040: In parallel, there have been efforts to extend the definition of the spectrum functor from the category of commutative rings to the category of rings.
elements.sent-2411-041: A robust notion of noncommutative spectrum should possess, at the very minimum, the following properties.
elements.sent-2411-042: The spectrum of every nonzero ring is nonempty.
elements.sent-2411-043: The spectrum, viewed in the appropriate framework, is a contravariant functor from the category of rings to the category of sets.
elements.sent-2411-044: These two properties are usually required to hold in conjunction with the extension property.
elements.sent-2411-045: The notion of spectrum defined on the category of all rings must agree with the Grothendieck spectrum of prime ideals when restricted to commutative rings.
elements.sent-2411-046: Goldman’s prime torsion theories~\cite{Goldman and the ``left spectrum'' of Rosenberg~\cite{rosenberg are examples of extensions of the spectrum of prime ideals for which only property (A) is satisfied.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: Examples of approaches that satisfy property (B) are the spectrum of the ``abelianization'' $R \mapsto \Spec(R/[R, R])$, the set of completely prime ideals, and the ``field spectrum'' of Cohn~\cite{Cohn .}
elements.sent-2411-048: In fact, it turns out that any extension of the Grothendieck spectrum of primes ideals leads to a notion of noncommutative spectrum that can only satisfy either property (A) or property (B) but not both.
elements.sent-2411-049: This was shown quite recently by Reyes in~\cite{nogo , rigorously demonstrating that a functor satisfying the properties (A), (B) and (*) cannot exist.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Therefore, if one wants to successfully extend the notion of spectrum to noncommutative rings, a different and, in a way, more sophisticated approach is needed.
elements.sent-2411-051: In \cite{nogo , Reyes has proved a similar no-go theorem for $C^*$-algebras and the Gelfand spectrum.}
elements.sent-2411-052: In the present paper, we focus on the algebraic setting, leaving for future work a similar study for $C^*$-algebras (and more general algebras) in the aforementioned bornological HAG framework of \cite{bambi2 and \cite{BKK .}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The goal of this paper is to define a sufficiently robust notion of noncommutative spectrum that allows one to implement a noncommutative Gelfand duality.
elements.sent-2411-054: We should immediately emphasise that the most appropriate framework in which to develop a theory of noncommutative schemes would be that of $\infty$-categories.
elements.sent-2411-055: However, in this paper we use derived and homotopy categories, instead.
elements.sent-2411-056: The main underlying reason for this choice is that we would like to make our paper accessible to a rather diverse readership, a large portion of which may not be familiar with $\infty$-categories\footnote{Readers with a background in the theory of $\infty$-categories will have no problem in translating our results in that framework.. .}
elements.sent-2411-057: Indeed, the duality we will be developing sits at the intersection of many different areas of mathematics, ranging from the very pure to the more applied, and different communities may find our results useful.
elements.sent-2411-058: As a final disclaimer, in this manuscript we only consider the categories of rings $\bRings_\Z$ and homotopical rings $\bH \bRings_\Z$, postponing until a future publication the more technically demanding case of Banach $C^*$-algebras.
elements.sent-2411-059: The first thing to notice is that although the statement of Theorem~\ref{thm:main is phrased in classical terms, it appears to be unreachable working within such framework.}
elements.sent-2411-060: The main reason why one is forced to go beyond the category of rings $\bRings_\Z$ is that the spectrum of noncommutative rings have open subsets whose natural algebra of functions is a differential graded algebra (dg-algebra), unlike the commutative case where such open subsets do not exist.
elements.sent-2411-061: A similar phenomenon happens in analytic geometry, and its study recently led to the resolution to the so-called sheafyness problem for Huber spaces \cite{bambi (a similar solution has been give also by Clausen and Scholze).}
elements.sent-2411-062: If we denote by $\bH\bRings_\Z := \bHo(\bD\bG\bA_\Z^{\le 0 )$ the homotopy category of connective dg-algebras over $\Z$, our main result shows Gelfand duality for $\bH\bRings_\Z$; the classical formulation of Theorem~\ref{thm:main is then obtained by restricting the latter via the canonical fully faithful inclusion $\bRings_\Z \subset \bH\bRings_\Z$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: We would like to stress that the data needed to obtain such a faithful representation of a ring on a topological space (\ie the structure pre-sheaf of functions on the spectrum) is not concentrated in degree $0$ even if we start with a ring concentrated in degree $0$ (see Example~\ref{exa:homotopy_epimorphism for more details on this).}
elements.sent-2411-064: We also observe that if one accepts to use $\bH\bRings_\Z$ as the basic category to study, then our approach represents one of the easiest and most natural ways to develop a geometric study of this category.
elements.sent-2411-065: The (anti-)equivalence of Theorem~\ref{thm:main is obtained as a two-step procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-066: First, we construct a functor that assigns to any connective dg-algebra $R\in\bH\bRings_\Z$ a topological space $\SpecNC(R)$, that we call noncommutative spectrum, and to any algebra homomorphism a continuous map of the associated topological spaces.
elements.sent-2411-067: Then we construct a homotopical pre-sheaf of connective dg-algebras on $\SpecNC(R)$, so that the global section $\mathcal{O _X(\SpecNC(R))$ is isomorphic to $R$ in $\bH\bRings_\Z$.}
elements.sent-2411-068: Combining these two steps, we obtain a contravariant duality functor \begin{equation}\Gelf : \bH\bRings_\Z \to PreRingSp\end{equation} that we call noncommutative Gelfand duality.
elements.sent-2411-069: By this we mean that the functor $\Gelf$ is a faithful functor.
elements.sent-2411-070: This is reminiscent of how affine schemes can be described as a (non-full) subcategory of $\mathbf{PreRingSp}$.
elements.sent-2411-071: We elaborate further on this matter in Section~\ref{sec:gelfand , especially on our use of pre-ringed spaces instead of (classical) ringed spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Our notion of spectrum, although formally similar the Grothendieck spectrum of a commutative ring, is new and does not usually agree with it, unless very specific cases are considered, like fields and commutative discrete valuation rings (see Section~\ref{exa:spectra ).}
elements.sent-2411-073: However, given a commutative ring, it is always possible to define a canonical map of pre-ringed spaces\footnote{Actually, for commutative rings we have that $\SpecNC(R)$ is a ringed space --- see Proposition~\ref{prop:sheafy .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: Remarkably, it is possible to define a relative version of our spectrum that agrees with Grothendieck spectrum for finitely generated commutative $\mathbb{C}$-algebras (see Subsection~\ref{sec:relative spectrum ).}
elements.sent-2411-075: To achieve our goal, we had to overcome a series of difficulties.
elements.sent-2411-076: The first consists in devising a Grothendieck topology suitable for noncommutative rings.
elements.sent-2411-077: In analogy with the classical Zariski topology of scheme theory, we defined what we call the formal homotopy Zariski topology on $\bdAff_\Z=\bH\bRings_\Z^\op$ (see Defition~\ref{defn:homotopical_Zariski_open_immersion ).}
elements.sent-2411-078: It is a highly non-trivial fact that such a topology even exists.
elements.sent-2411-079: Natural candidates for generalisations of the Zariski topology in $\bRings_\Z^\op$ fail to be suitable classes of maps.
elements.sent-2411-080: In the category of commutative rings $\bC\bRings_\Z$, the Zariski topology is defined by the class of flat epimorphisms of finite presentation.
elements.sent-2411-081: But flat maps are not stable by pushouts in $\bRings_\Z$, thus cannot be used to define a topology.
elements.sent-2411-082: On the other hand, the class of all epimorphisms of rings obviously defines a topology but it is too large, encompassing morphisms that do not correspond to Zariski open immersions of classical affine schemes.
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore, the task is to find a class of morphisms that is not quite flat but close to be flat, such that in the commutative case it reduces to the class of flat epimorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-084: Surprisingly, such a class of morphisms does exist in $\bH\bRings_\Z$.
elements.sent-2411-085: This is the class of homotopical epimorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-086: Similarly to the class of epimorphisms in $\bRings_\Z$, the class of homotopical epimorphisms is suitable to define a topology on $\bH\bRings_\Z$.
elements.sent-2411-087: Moreover, the computations of \cite{Laz2 show that the commutative and noncommutative notions of homotopical epimorphisms are compatible.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Even more, \cite{TV3 shows that a morphism between discrete commutative rings is a flat epimorphism of finite presentation if and only if it is a homotopical epimorphism of finite presentation.}
elements.sent-2411-089: Let us now move to the second main obstacle.
elements.sent-2411-090: In all our previous considerations, we have been using morphisms of finite presentation.
elements.sent-2411-091: It turns out that the notions of finite presentation in the commutative and in the noncommutative settings are fundamentally incompatible.
elements.sent-2411-092: We will devote Subsection~\ref{On the notion of finite presentation to clarifying this matter.}
elements.sent-2411-093: To circumvent such compatibility issue, unlike for the classical Zariski topology, in our definition we do not require the localization morphisms to be of finite presentation.
elements.sent-2411-094: Dropping this requirement turns out to be essential to obtain a meaningful spectrum out of our topology on $\bdAff_\Z$.
elements.sent-2411-095: Indeed, if one assumes the finite presentation of the localization morphisms in $\bH \bRings_\Z$, then the topological space obtained as spectrum will be just a singleton for most commutative rings (see Proposition~\ref{prop:wrong_spectrum ).}
elements.sent-2411-096: Using the homotopical Zariski topology briefly outlined above, one can associate to any fixed object $A \in \bH\bRings_\Z$ the (essentially small) category of localizations (\ie homotopical epimorphism) starting from $A$.
elements.sent-2411-097: This category is, in fact, a poset that agrees with the poset of smashing localizations of $\bH \bMod_A$ (see Proposition \ref{prop:iso_smashing_poset ).}
elements.sent-2411-098: This kind of posets have already been considered in the literature, mainly in the context of triangulated categories.
elements.sent-2411-099: One of the main observations about them is that these posets tend not to be distributive when the ring $A$ is not commutative.
elements.sent-2411-100: Therefore, it is not clear how to use them to obtain a topological space in a similar fashion to how Stone's duality associates a spectral topological space to any distributive lattice.
elements.sent-2411-101: In our setting, we have the extra datum of the homotopical Zariski topology, which endows the lattice of homotopical epimorphisms with a notion of coverage that allows one to canonically associate to it a sober topological space.
elements.sent-2411-102: The topological space thus obtained is denoted by $\SpecNC(A)$, is functorially associated to $A$ and always non-empty (actually, often of huge size).
elements.sent-2411-103: In Section~\ref{exa:spectra we provide a description of the topological space $\SpecNC(A)$ for some key examples, both for commutative and noncommutative rings.}
elements.sent-2411-104: This bring us to the last hurdle we faced in proving Theorem~\ref{thm:main .}
elements.sent-2411-105: Already in the setting of the theory of affine schemes, the topological space $\Spec_G(A)$ does not contain enough information to allow one to reconstruct the ring from which it originated.
elements.sent-2411-106: The solution to this problem is to add to $\Spec_G(A)$ the extra datum of a structure sheaf of rings.
elements.sent-2411-107: One would like to perform the same enrichment for the topological space $\SpecNC(A)$, but it turns out that for noncommutative rings the structure presheaf is not a sheaf (see Example~\ref{exa:structure_not_sheaf ).}
elements.sent-2411-108: We do not view this as a shortcoming of our construction, but actually as an interesting feature deserving deeper study and understanding.
elements.sent-2411-109: Indeed, although the structure sheaf is not a sheaf in the usual sense, it still satisfies a form of comonadic descent that is reminiscent of the sheaf condition, and actually reduces to it in the commutative case.
elements.sent-2411-110: Therefore, the structure pre-sheaf belong to a new class of pre-sheaves that we call descendable, whose sections have the property of being reconstructible from their localizations on a cover, although this reconstruction procedure does not agree with the sheaf theoretic procedure in general.
elements.sent-2411-111: We will devote Section~\ref{sec:gelfand to the study of this issue and to the proof of Gelfand duality.}
elements.sent-2411-112: We would like to point out that the Gelfand duality obtained in Theorem~\ref{thm:main is not yet a perfect duality, and there is room for improvement.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Although we have shown that the category of affine derived noncommutative schemes $\bH\bRings_\Z^\op$ is equivalent to a subcategory of $\mathbf{PreRingSp}$, we have not explicitly described the essential image of the duality functor $\Gelf$.
elements.sent-2411-115: This is analogous to describing the category of affine schemes as a non-full subcategory of the category of ringed spaces.
elements.sent-2411-116: In the latter situation, it has been realised that affine schemes embeds fully faithfully into the category of locally ringed spaces, giving a precise description of the essential image of the duality functor for affine schemes.
elements.sent-2411-117: We explain this matter in Section~\ref{sec:gelfand .}
elements.sent-2411-118: The study of the essential image of the functor $\Gelf$ will be addressed in a separate work.
elements.sent-2411-119: The paper is organised as follows.
elements.sent-2411-120: Section \ref{sec:preliminaries contains some background material and fixes the notation used in the rest of the paper.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Section \ref{sec:homotopy_algebras recalls materials from homotopical algebra and, in particular, studies the differences (and incompatibility) between the notions of finite presentation of an algebra in the commutative and in the noncommutative settings.}
elements.sent-2411-122: This incompatibility has profound implications on the relationship between the commutative and noncommutative versions of the theory of schemes.
elements.sent-2411-123: In Section~\ref{sec:spectrum we define the homotopical Zariski topology on $\bH \bRings_\Z^\op$ and explain how to construct a spectrum functor out of this datum.}
elements.sent-2411-124: In Section~\ref{sec:properties_spectrum we study some basic properties of the spectrum functor obtained previouslt and compare it with other notions present in literature.}
elements.sent-2411-125: In particular, we show that our spectrum always projects onto Grothendieck's spectrum of prime ideals when the ring is commutative, and that it can be computed using the lattice of smashing subcategories of the category of (indifferently, left or right) modules.
elements.sent-2411-126: In Section~\ref{exa:spectra we provide some explicit computations of the topological space $\SpecNC(A)$ for some key examples, both commutative and noncommutative.}
elements.sent-2411-127: In Section~\ref{sec:gelfand we examine the issue of descent of modules for the homotopical Zariski topology.}
elements.sent-2411-128: We show that modules can be reconstructed from their localizations using the machinery of comonadic descent.
elements.sent-2411-129: This, combined with the observation that the structure sheaf is not a sheaf for noncommutative rings, leads to the observation that the classical notion of sheaf is not adequate for noncommutative geometry and some sort of generalisation thereof is needed to progress further.
elements.sent-2411-130: We provide a first attempt in this direction and verify that our result is consistent with the theory developed in the commutative case.
elements.sent-2411-131: This leads us to a duality theory for $\bH \bRings_\Z$ that generalises and is compatible with the duality between affine schemes and commutative rings.

elements.sent-2411-137: We collect here standard notions from category theory in an abridged fashion, mostly to settle notation, and refer the reader to \cite{Mac, Wei for further details.}
elements.sent-2411-138: If $\sC$ is a category, we use the same symbol, $\sC$, to denote the class of objects of $\sC$, so that $X \in \sC$ means that $X$ is an object of $\sC$.
elements.sent-2411-139: We denote by $\id_\sC: \sC \to \sC$ the {\it identity functor .}
elements.sent-2411-140: For an object $X \in \sC$ we denote by $\sC/X$ the category of {\it objects over $X$} and by $X/\sC$ the category of {\it objects under $X$}.
elements.sent-2411-141: For two objects $X, Y \in \sC$ we denote by $\Hom_{\sC (X, Y)$ the set of morphisms between them.}
elements.sent-2411-143: If the category to which the hom-sets are referring to is clear from the context, we drop the $\sC$ and simply write $\Hom(X, Y)$.
elements.sent-2411-144: A {\it monoidal structure on a category $\sC$ is the data of a functor $\ootimes: \sC \times \sC \to \sC$ and an object $1_\ootimes \in \sC$, equipped with natural isomorphisms $1_\ootimes \ootimes X \stackrel{\cong {\to X$, $X \ootimes 1_\ootimes \stackrel{\cong {\to X$ and $(X \ootimes Y) \ootimes Z \stackrel{\cong {\to X \ootimes (Y \ootimes Z)$, for objects $X, Y, Z \in \sC$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-145: The natural isomorphisms are required to satisfy appropriate coherence relations that we omit for the sake of brevity.
elements.sent-2411-146: The object $1_\ootimes$ is called the {\it identity (or {\it unit ) of the monoidal structure $\ootimes$.}}
elements.sent-2411-147: The data of a category with a choice of a monoidal structure $(\sC, \ootimes, 1_\ootimes)$ is called {\it monoidal category .}
elements.sent-2411-148: If we also require the datum of a natural isomorphism $X \ootimes Y \stackrel{\cong {\to Y \ootimes X$, satisfying some further coherence relations, then the monoidal structure is called {\it symmetric and $(\sC, \ootimes, 1_\ootimes)$ is called {\it symmetric monoidal category .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-149: Note that the symmetry condition amounts to prescribing extra data.
elements.sent-2411-150: In this paper we will work with several symmetric monoidal categories.
elements.sent-2411-151: The most basic example of a symmetric monoidal category relevant to our work is the category of abelian groups $\bAb$ equipped with the standard tensor product $\otimes_\Z$.
elements.sent-2411-152: Another basic example is the category of sets $\bSets$ equipped with the binary direct product $\prod$.
elements.sent-2411-153: Given a monoidal category $(\sC, \ootimes, 1_\ootimes)$ we define the category of {\it algebras (also called {\it monoids ) over it, denoted by $\bAlg(\sC)$, as follows.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The objects of $\bAlg(\sC)$ are objects $X \in \sC$ equipped with a morphism $m: X \ootimes X \to X$, called {\it multiplication , and a morphism $\eta: 1_\ootimes \to X$, called {\it identity , satisfying the usual relations (written in a diagrammatic form).}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Morphisms in $\bAlg(\sC)$ are morphisms of the underlying objects of $\sC$ that commute with multiplication and identities.
elements.sent-2411-156: If $\ootimes$ is symmetric, then we can also consider the category of {\it commutative algebras (or {\it commutative monoids ), denoted by $\bCAlg(\sC)$, namely the full subcategory of $\bAlg(\sC)$ of objects whose multiplication map commutes with the symmetric natural transformation of $\ootimes$.}}
elements.sent-2411-157: For example, if we consider $(\bAb, \otimes_\Z)$, then $\bAlg(\bAb)$ is the category of rings and $\bCAlg(\bAb)$ is the category of commutative rings, whereas if we consider $(\bSets, \prod)$ then $\bAlg(\bSets)$ is the category of monoids and $\bCAlg(\bSets)$ is the category of commutative monoids.
elements.sent-2411-158: To any object $A \in \bAlg(\sC)$ we associate the corresponding category of left (resp.~right) modules ${ _A \bMod$ (resp.~$\bMod_A$) as follows.}
elements.sent-2411-159: Objects of ${ _A \bMod$ are objects $M \in \sC$ equipped with an action $A \ootimes M \to M$ and morphisms are morphisms of $\sC$ commuting with the action.}
elements.sent-2411-160: If $\ootimes$ is symmetric and $A$ is commutative (\ie, $A\in \bCAlg(\sC)$), then ${ _A \bMod \cong \bMod_A$ canonically and $\bMod_A$ has an induced symmetric monoidal structure $\ootimes_A$.}
elements.sent-2411-162: By $\bRings$ we denote the category of rings with identity-preserving homomorphisms as morphisms, while the full subcategory of commutative rings will be denoted by $\bC\bRings$.
elements.sent-2411-164: For a ring $R \in \bRings$ we call the category $\bRings_R := R/\bRings$ the category of {\it $R$-algebras and by $\bMod_R$ the category of (right) modules over a ring $R \in \bRings$.}
elements.sent-2411-165: We denote by $\bCh(\bMod_R)$ the category of (unbounded) {\it chain complexes over $\bMod_R$ and by $\bCh^{\le 0 (\bMod_R) \subset \bCh(\bMod_R)$ the category of {\it connective complexes , \ie, complexes concentrated in negative degrees (we use the cohomological convention for differentials, \ie~differentials increase the degree).}}}

elements.sent-2411-168: Algebras over $\bCh(\bMod_R)$ for this monoidal structure are called\footnote{Here ``dg" stands for ``Differential Graded".}
elements.sent-2411-169: Concretely, a dg-algebra over $R$ is a complex of $R$-modules $A^\bullet$ together with a graded multiplication $A^n \otimes A^m \to A^{n +m}$, satisfying the Leibniz rule \begin{equation}d(a b) = d(a) b + (-1)^n a d(b)\end{equation} for $a \in A^n, b \in A^m$.
elements.sent-2411-170: A dg-algebra is commutative if its multiplication is graded commutative, \ie~if \begin{equation}a b = (-1)^{n m} b a\end{equation} for all $a \in A^n, b \in A^m$.
elements.sent-2411-171: We denote by $\Delta$ the simplex category, namely the category of finite sets where each set $[n]:= \{ 0, \ldots, n \}$ is equipped with the canonical total ordering $0 < 1 < \ldots < n$ and where $\Hom_\Delta([n], [m])$ consists only of non-decreasing maps for this ordering.
elements.sent-2411-172: A functor $\Delta^\op \to \sC$ is called a simplicial object in $\sC$ and a functor $\Delta \to \sC$ is called a cosimplicial object in $\sC$.
elements.sent-2411-173: The class of simplicial and cosimplicial objects in a category is organised into a category whose morphisms are natural transformations of functors.
elements.sent-2411-174: In general we will denote the category of simplicial objects in $\sC$ by $\bS \sC$.

elements.sent-2411-177: As per the above, we denote by $\bSMod_R$ the category of simplicial objects in $\bMod_R$, \ie~the category of functors $\Delta^\op \to \bMod_R$.

elements.sent-2411-182: The equivalence is realised by an adjoint pair of functors \begin{equation}N: \bS\bMod_R \leftrightarrows \bCh^{\le 0}(\bMod_R): \Gamma\,,\end{equation} where $N$ is called the {\it normalised complex functor and $\Gamma$ the {\it nerve functor .}}
elements.sent-2411-183: We will not be defining the functors $N$ and $\Gamma$ explicitly here, in that we will only be relying on some of their formal properties.
elements.sent-2411-184: Their most important property is that, remarkably, $\Gamma$ sends quasi-isomorphisms of complexes to weak equivalences of simplicial set and, dually, $N$ sends weak equivalences to quasi-isomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-185: This property implies that one can compute the cohomology of $X \in \bCh^{\le 0 (\bMod_R)$ as the homotopy groups of $\Gamma(X)$, and, viceversa, one can compute the homotopy groups of $S \in \bS\bMod_R$ as the cohomology of the complex $N(S)$.}

elements.sent-2411-187: Another important property of the Dold--Kan correspondence is that, when $R$ is commutative, the functors $N$ and $\Gamma$ are both lax monoidal and oplax monoidal functors, \ie~there exist natural transformations \begin{equation}\Delta_{S, T}: N(S) \otimes_R N(T) \to N(S \otimes_R T), \qquad \ \nabla_{S, T}: N(S \otimes_R T) \to N(S) \otimes_R N (T)\end{equation} for all $S, T \in \bS\bMod_R$ called the Eilenberg-Zibler map and the Alexander-Whitney map, respectively, which, by formal properties of adjoint functors, induce natural transformations \begin{equation}\Gamma(X) \otimes_R \Gamma(Y) \to \Gamma(X \otimes_R Y), \qquad \ \Gamma(S \otimes_R T) \to \Gamma(S) \otimes_R \Gamma(T).\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-194: Let us briefly recall some notions from homotopy theory needed in this work.
elements.sent-2411-195: Rather than using the language of $\infty$-categories, which would be preferable but is less accessible, we will work here, for simplicity, in the framework of model categories.
elements.sent-2411-196: A model structure on a category $\sC$ is the datum of three distinguished classes $(W, F, C)$ of morphisms of $\sC$, called weak equivalences, fibrations and cofibrations, respectively, satisfying appropriate conditions, for which we refer the reader to \cite[Definition 1.1.3]{Hov .}
elements.sent-2411-197: For the reader less accustomed to model categories, it may be helpful to think about weak equivalences as the analogue of (weak) `homotopy equivalences', fibrations as the analogue `well-behaved surjections' and cofibrations as analogue of `well-behaved inclusions'.
elements.sent-2411-198: Of these three classes of maps the most important are weak equivalences $W$, in that $W$ is used to define the homotopy category of $\sC$.
elements.sent-2411-199: The latter is defined as $\bHo(\sC):=\sC[W^{-1 ]$, \ie~ as the category obtained from $\sC$ by formally inverting the morphisms in the class $W$.}
elements.sent-2411-200: The other two classes are additional data used for computing objects and morphisms in $\sC[W^{-1 ]$.}
elements.sent-2411-201: Let us emphasise that the notion of model structure contains redundant information.
elements.sent-2411-202: For example, we will use the fact that the classes $W$ and $F$ uniquely determine $C$ (and, vice versa, that $W$ and $C$ determine $F$).
elements.sent-2411-203: A {\it model category is a complete and cocomplete category $\sC$ equipped with a model structure $(W,F,C)$.}
elements.sent-2411-204: We recall that morphisms belonging to $W \cap F$ are called trivial fibrations and to $W \cap C$ are called trivial cofibrations.
elements.sent-2411-205: Also, an object $X \in \sC$ such that the morphism from the initial object $\void \to X$ is a cofibration is called cofibrant, whereas an object from which the canonical morphism to the final object $X \to 0$ is a fibration is called fibrant.
elements.sent-2411-206: Given two model categories $\sC$ and $\sD$, a pair of adjoint functors $(F\dashv G): \sC \leftrightarrows \sD$ is said to be a Quillen adjunction if $F$ preserves cofibrations and acyclic cofibrations (or, equivalently, if $G$ preserves fibrations and acyclic fibrations).

elements.sent-2411-289: For $R \in \bC \bRings$, let us denote by $R\langle x_1, \ldots, x_n\rangle$ the free algebra with coefficients in $R$, with the property that $R$ is in the centre of the algebra.

elements.sent-2411-317: Note that an object $A \in \bRings_R$ is of finite presentation if and only if the structure map $R \to A$ is a morphism of finite presentation, and a morphism $A \to B$ is of finite presentation if and only if $B$ is an object of finite presentation in $\bRings_A$.
elements.sent-2411-318: The same is true for commutative rings.
elements.sent-2411-319: Therefore, when it comes to finite presentation, the use of the terminology for morphisms and objects is compatible.
elements.sent-2411-320: Henceforth, we will often tacitly identify the two notions.
elements.sent-2411-321: The notions of finite presentation in the commutative and noncommutative cases are formally the same.
elements.sent-2411-322: Therefore, it is natural to ask whether the two are compatible; namely, whether a morphism in $\bC\bRings_R$ is of finite presentation if and only if it is of finite presentation as a morphism in $\bRings_R$.
elements.sent-2411-323: Perhaps surprisingly, this matter turns out to be very subtle, and the answer is in the negative, as the following example demonstrates.
elements.sent-2411-324: Clearly, $S$ is commutative and the canonical inclusion morphism $R \to S$ is of finite presentation in $\bC \bRings_R$, since $S$ is a free finitely generated commutative $R$-algebra.
elements.sent-2411-325: As an object in $\bRings_R$, $S$ can be realised as the quotient of $ R \{ y \} = \Z\langle y\rangle \ast_\Z R $ by the ideal \begin{equation}I:=\{ ( x_j y - y x_j ) \ |\ j\in \N \}.\end{equation}. Namely, \begin{equation}S \cong \frac{R \{ y \}}{I}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-326: Let us emphasise that the variable $y$ of $R \{ y \}$ does not commute with the variables $x_j$, $j\in \N$, of $R$.
elements.sent-2411-327: We claim that the morphism $f:R \to S$ is not of finite presentation in $\bRings_R$.
elements.sent-2411-328: Indeed, for $n\in \N$ define $I_n:=\{ ( x_j y - y x_j ) \in I \ |\ j\le n \}$.
elements.sent-2411-329: Then the canonical map \begin{equation}\underset{n \in \N}{\limind} \Hom_{\bRings_R} \left (S, \frac{R\{ y \}}{I_n} \right) \to \Hom_{\bRings_R} \left(S, \underset{n \in \N}\limind \frac{ R\{ y \}}{I_n} \right ) \cong \Hom_{\bRings_R} \left(S, S \right )\end{equation} fails to be an isomorphism because the identity $S \to S$ does not factor through any of the quotients on the left-hand-side.
elements.sent-2411-330: In particular, this shows that $S$ is not finitely presented as an object in $\bRings_R$, recall Remark~\ref{remark confuse mor and obj .}
elements.sent-2411-331: Now, in Example~\ref{exa:non_finitely_generated_free_algebras the (commutative) ring $R$ does not map into the center of $R\{y\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-332: This leads to a violation of the finite presentation property --- as soon as $R$ is not finitely generated --- because in order to write $R[y]$ as a quotient of $R \{y \}$ one must add infinitely many relations to ensure that in the resulting quotient $y$ commutes with all elements.
elements.sent-2411-333: There are (at least) two possible ways to reconcile the notions of finite presentation in the commutative and noncommutative settings.
elements.sent-2411-334: One way is to restrict oneself to the case where both $R$ and $R\{y\}$ (or more general version thereof) are finitely generated over $\Z$; for example, this holds for discrete algebras (also known as $\Z$-algebras).
elements.sent-2411-335: Another way is to consider the category of $R$-algebras for which $R$ maps into their centre.
elements.sent-2411-336: The latter opens up an intermediate situation which we will now examine.

elements.subsubsection-2411-002: Reformulation in terms of algebraic theories
elements.sent-2411-356: The best way to explain the incompatibility described so far between the notions of finite presentation is perhaps to think in terms of algebraic theories in the sense of Lawvere~\cite{Lawvere .}
elements.sent-2411-357: In this subsection we briefly explain how this is done.
elements.sent-2411-358: The algebraic category of $\sC$ is the full subcategory of the category of pre-sheaves on $\sC$ \begin{equation}\bP_\Sigma(\sC) = \bFunc^\times(\sC^\op, \bSets) \subset \bFunc(\sC^\op, \bSets) = \bPsh(\sC)\end{equation} where $\bFunc^\times$ denotes the class of functors that preserve finite products (of $\sC^\op$).
elements.sent-2411-359: Therefore, objects of $\bFunc^\times(\sC^\op, \bSets)$ are contravariant functors $\sF: \sC^\op \to \bSets$ such that the canonical morphism \begin{equation}\sF(A \coprod B) \to \sF(A) \times \sF(B)\end{equation} is an isomorphism.
elements.sent-2411-360: In the same way it is possible to show that objects of $\bPsh(\sC)$ can be written as arbitrary colimits in $\bPsh(\sC)$ of representable pre-sheaves, one can also show that objects of $\bP_\Sigma(\sC)$ are precisely those pre-sheaves that can be written as sifted colimits\footnote{A colimit $\limind_{i \in I A_i$ is said sifted if the partially ordered set $I$ is such that the diagonal map $I \to I \times I$ is cofinal.}}
elements.sent-2411-361: For example, filtered colimits are sifted, but not all sifted colimits are filtered.. of representable pre-sheaves.
elements.sent-2411-362: For this reason, the reader should think of the objects of $\sC$ as the free and finitely generated objects of some class of algebraic structures and of the objects of $\bP_\Sigma(\sC)$ as obtained from the free ones by considering infinite unions and quotients by relations.
elements.sent-2411-363: Almost all categories of algebraic objects can be presented as $\bP_\Sigma(\sC)$ for a suitable $\sC$.
elements.sent-2411-364: We provide below some basic examples.. \begin{exa.}
elements.sent-2411-365: If $\sC$ is the category of finite sets, then $\bP_\Sigma(\sC)$ is the category of sets.
elements.sent-2411-366: If $\sC$ is the category of finitely generated and free abelian groups, \ie abelian groups of the form $\Z^n$ for $n \in \N$, then $\bP_\Sigma(\sC) \cong \bAb$.
elements.sent-2411-367: This is (roughly) equivalent to say that any abelian group can be written as a quotient of a free abelian group, \ie it has a presentation in terms of (possibly infinitely many) generators and relations.
elements.sent-2411-368: The previous example can be generalized immediately to left (or right) modules over any ring.
elements.sent-2411-369: Indeed, if $R$ is any ring and $\sC$ is the category of finitely generated and free left (or right) modules then $\bP_\Sigma(\sC) \cong { _R \bMod$ (resp.. $\bP_\Sigma(\sC) \cong \bMod_R$).}

elements.sent-2411-471: This section is the core of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-472: Using derived geometry, we will introduce a robust notion of spectrum for homotopical rings.
elements.sent-2411-473: This will be achieved by defining a Grothendieck topology on the category $\bH\bRings_\Z^\op$, in analogy with the Zariski topology used in scheme theory.
elements.sent-2411-474: We will argue that $\bH \bRings_\Z^\op$ is a good candidate for a noncommutative replacement of the category of affine schemes.
elements.sent-2411-475: Denoting by $\bdAff_\Z:=\bH\bRings_\Z^\op$ the category of derived noncommutative affine schemes, let us introduce the formal duality functor (\ie~the formal contravariant functor mapping an object to itself and a morphism to the opposite morphism) \begin{equation}\label{Spec formal} Spec : \;\bH\bRings_\Z \to \bdAff_\Z \nonumber A \mapsto \Spec(A)=:X\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-476: In the remainder of this section we will provide a geometric construction of the functor $\Spec$ that closely mimics the classical construction of the spectrum of a commutative ring given by Grothendieck.
elements.sent-2411-477: Namely, we will define a Grothendieck topology on $\bdAff_\Z$ that almost precisely generalises the (classical) Zariski topology of scheme theory.

elements.sent-2411-497: In order to define a Grothendieck topology on $\bdAff_\Z$ we first need to specify appropriate notions of open embedding and cover for the topology.

elements.sent-2411-539: We now want to associate to any object $R \in \bH\bRings_\Z$ a geometric space using the homotopy Zariski topology on $\bH\bRings_R$.
elements.sent-2411-540: The procedure we adopt here is the same that can be used to obtain the Grothendieck spectrum of a commutative ring from the data of the classical Zariski topology on the category of affine schemes\footnote{In Proposition \ref{prop:compatision with Grothendieck we will give more details about this procedure in the classical setting.. .}}
elements.sent-2411-541: To do this we introduce the following notation.
elements.sent-2411-542: We define $\bLoc(R)$, the category of localizations of $R$, to be the subcategory of all homotopy epimorphisms in $\bH\bRings_R$ whose domain is $R$.
elements.sent-2411-543: We define $\bOuv(X)$, the category of open immersions of $X$, to be the subcategory of all homotopy monomorphisms in $\bdAff_R$ whose codomain is $X$.
elements.sent-2411-544: Before moving forward with the study of the categories $\bLoc(R)$ and $\bOuv(X)$, let us discuss some examples of formal homotopy Zariski localizations.. \begin{exa.}
elements.sent-2411-546: Let $R$ be a (discrete) commutative ring and consider a Zariski localization, namely a morphism of commutative rings $R \to S$ that induces an open embedding $\Spec(S) \to \Spec(R)$ for the classical Zariski topology.
elements.sent-2411-547: As shown in \cite[Lemma 2.1.4 (1)]{TV3 , a Zariski localization induces a homotopical epimorphism in $\bH\bRings_R$.}
elements.sent-2411-548: For the sake of completeness, let us mention that the family of Zariski localizations can be also characterised algebraically as the family of flat epimorphisms of finite presentation.
elements.sent-2411-549: Notice also that \cite[Lemma 2.1.4]{TV3 shows that the homotopy Zariski topology on commutative simplicial rings precisely restricts to the classical Zariski topology on discrete commutative rings.}
elements.sent-2411-550: In this sense, the homotopical Zariski topology is a generalization of the classical Zariski topology of scheme theory.
elements.sent-2411-551: A generalisation of the previous example is the class of flat epimorphisms between (discrete) rings $f: R \to S$.
elements.sent-2411-552: This class encompasses the class of Zariski localizations and although it is a priori a huge class, it is well-known that the family of isomorphism classes (in the category of $R$-algebras) of epimorphisms with fixed source ring $R$ is a set.
elements.sent-2411-553: The sub-class of flat ring epimorphisms has been thoroughly studied in the literature and, by definition, is a sub-class of homotopical epimorphisms of ring.
elements.sent-2411-554: Indeed, if $S$ is flat over $R$, then the canonical map $S \otimes_R^\L S \to S \otimes_R S$ is an equivalence; since $f$ is an epimorphism, $S \otimes_R S \to S$ is an isomorphism, and the fact that the composition $S \otimes_R^\L S \to S \otimes_R S \to S$ is an equivalence is precisely the request of $R \to S$ being a homotopical epimorphism.
elements.sent-2411-555: The class of flat ring epimorphism is a nice and easy-to-study class of morphisms --- a natural candidate for a noncommutative generalisation of the class of Zariski localizations.
elements.sent-2411-556: However, at closer inspection one realises that this class cannot be used in the definition of a Grothendieck topology for noncommutative rings, because it is not stable under push-outs of rings.
elements.sent-2411-557: To see this, consider the following situation.
elements.sent-2411-558: Let $R$ be a ring and $s \in R$ be such that the multiplicative subset $\{1, s, s^2, \ldots \} \subset R$ is not an Ore subset and the localization $R \to R[s^{-1 ]$ is not flat.}

elements.sent-2411-664: In preparation for this, let us recall the following definition from topos theory.
elements.sent-2411-665: An ideal of $(S, \tau)$ is a subset $I \subset S$ such that.

elements.sent-2411-667: Furthermore, all spectral topological spaces can be written as the frame of ideals of some distributive lattice.
elements.sent-2411-668: Then $\SpecNC(A)$ is the sober topological space associated with the frame of ideals $\Id(\bZar_X)$.

elements.sent-2411-673: The poset $\bZar_X$ and its topology contain all the information required to construct the topological space $\SpecNC(A)$ (in concrete terms, it contains a base for the topology).
elements.sent-2411-674: In particular, the frame $\Id(\bZar_X)$ is the frame of open subsets of $\SpecNC(A)$.
elements.sent-2411-675: A point $x \in \SpecNC(A)$ belongs to an open subset $U \subset \SpecNC(A)$ corresponding to an element $\cU \in \Id(\bZar_X)$ if and only if $\cU \in \sI_x$, where $\sI_x$ is the completely prime filter associated to $x$.
elements.sent-2411-676: This correspondence maps objects of $\Id(\bZar_X)$ to subsets of $\SpecNC(A)$, thus yielding an injective map $\Id(\bZar_X) \to \sP(\SpecNC(A))$ that intertwines the partial order in $\Id(\bZar_X)$ with the inclusion order in $\sP(\SpecNC(A))$.

elements.sent-2411-733: As we observed in Section \ref{Categories of algebras , to a given commutative ring $R$ one can associate two different categories of algebras under it: the category $\bRings_R$ of all $R$-algebras and the category $\bZ\bRings_R$ of central algebras over $R$.}
elements.sent-2411-734: The difference between the two categories can be understood very clearly in terms of algebraic theories: the category $\bRings_R$ is the algebraic category generated by the class of algebras \begin{equation}\{ \Z \lt x_1, \ldots, x_n \gt \ast_\Z R \}_{n \in \N},\end{equation} whereas $\bZ \bRings_R$ is the algebraic category generated by the class of algebras \begin{equation}\{ R \lt x_1, \ldots, x_n \gt \}_{n \in \N}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-735: So, in analogy with the homotopy theory of $\bRings_R$ presented so far, one can develop a homotopy theory of $\bZ\bRings_R$ without additional obstacles.

elements.subsection-2411-013: A few properties of the noncommutative spectrum
elements.sent-2411-806: We conclude this section by discussing a few basic properties of the new notion of spectrum defined above.
elements.sent-2411-807: If $A \cong A_1 \times A_2$ in $\bH\bRings_\Z$, then $\SpecNC(A) \cong \SpecNC(A_1) \coprod \SpecNC(A_2)$ as topological spaces.

elements.sent-2411-811: If $A, B \in \bRings_\Z$ are derived Morita equivalent, then $\SpecNC(A) \cong \SpecNC(B)$.

elements.sent-2411-827: The analysis performed in this paper lays the foundations of the study of noncommutative geometry over $\bH\bRings_\Z$.

elements.section-2411-007: Towards a noncommutative notion of sheaf: a descent problem
elements.sent-2411-899: Gelfand's duality realises a duality between the category of compact Hausdorff topological spaces and the category of commutative $C^*$-algebras.
elements.sent-2411-900: Some years after Gelfand's work, Grothendieck observed that, in order to obtain a similar duality for commutative rings, some extra data is needed.
elements.sent-2411-901: Indeed, for example, if we just regard the spectrum of a commutative ring as a topological space, we cannot tell any field apart from any other: all their spectra are isomorphic to the singleton topological space.
elements.sent-2411-902: The extra data required to realise a precise duality between commutative rings and their spectra is the structural sheaf of rings whose stalks at any point are local rings.
elements.sent-2411-903: The datum of a topological space equipped with such a structure sheaf is called a locally ringed space.
elements.sent-2411-904: Grothendieck has shown that the category of affine schemes embeds fully faithfully into the category of locally ringed spaces, realising an algebraic version of Gelfand's duality.
elements.sent-2411-905: Our goal is to achieve a similar result in the noncommutative setting using the notion of spectrum introduced above.
elements.sent-2411-906: Since we are in the homotopical setting, we would like to replace the classical notion of sheaf with its homotopical version commonly used in derived algebraic geometry.
elements.sent-2411-907: But this does not work: for noncommutative rings the structure pre-sheaf is not a sheaf --- not even a homotopical one.
elements.sent-2411-908: Nevertheless, we do not view this as a shortcoming of our theory! Instead, this is where interesting new mathematics is happening.
elements.sent-2411-909: In this section we start unravelling these new phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-910: Before doing so, let us analyse a simple explicit example of such a non-sheafy behaviour of the structure pre-sheaf for noncommutative rings, to guide our intuition.
elements.sent-2411-911: In subsection \ref{sec:noncommutative_spectra we have seen that $X = \SpecNC(A) = \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}$ is the discrete space with three points.}
elements.sent-2411-912: These three points, being open subsets, correspond to three homotopical epimorphisms $A \to k$, $A \to M_2(k)$ and $A \to k$ (see Example \ref{exa:A_2_descent below for more information about this).}
elements.sent-2411-913: Thus, the structural pre-sheaf is given by \begin{equation}\cO_X(p_1) = k, \ \ \cO_X(p_2) = M_2(k), \ \ \cO_X(p_3) = k.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-914: If this pre-sheaf were a sheaf, then one would have that the cover of $X$ made by singletons gives \begin{equation}\cO_X(X) = k \oplus M_2(k) \oplus k\,;\end{equation} but $\cO_X(X) = A$, and the latter has dimension $3$ as $k$-vector space.
elements.sent-2411-915: However, we know that the covers of $X$ for the homotopical Zariski topology induce faithful, hence conservative, functors at the level of base changes of modules (see Proposition \ref{prop:faithful_covers ).}
elements.sent-2411-916: We will show that this implies that such family of functors satisfy descent (in the homotopical sense).
elements.sent-2411-917: Therefore, there is still a local-to-global principle that the pre-sheaf $\cO_X$ and modules over it satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-918: A localization and reconstruction of the data of such pre-sheaves does not follow the rules of sheaf theory but those of comonadic descent (of which sheaf theory is a particular case).
elements.sent-2411-919: Let us elaborate on this point for the special case of the path algebra of the $A_2$ quiver, before addressing the abstract theory.
elements.sent-2411-920: The algebra $A$ over a field $k$ is isomorphic to the algebra of lower triangular matrices of the form \begin{equation}A =  k 0 k k .\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-921: Let us denote a $k$-basis of $A$ by $e_1, e_2, e_3$, so that \begin{equation}A =  k e_1 0 k e_2 k e_3 .\end{equation}. We denote \begin{equation}P_1 = k e_3, \ \ P_2 = k e_1 \oplus k e_2, \ \ S_2 = k e_1.\end{equation}.

elements.formula-2411-163: \begin{equation}P_1 = k e_3, \ \ P_2 = k e_1 \oplus k e_2, \ \ S_2 = k e_1.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-922: Note that $P_1$ and $P_2$ are projective left $A$-modules, $A \cong P_1 \oplus P_2$ as a left $A$-module, whereas $S_2$ has the left $A$-module structure induced by the quotient map $A \to S_2$ and it is obviously indecomposable.

elements.sent-2411-956: In the remainder of this section, we shall partially develop the abstract theory.
elements.sent-2411-957: We shall limit ourselves to the algebraic aspects of the theory here, deferring the development of a fully fledged geometric theory until future work.
elements.sent-2411-958: The prototype of the result we have in mind is \cite[Proposition 10.5]{Scholze .}
elements.sent-2411-959: Let us immediately warn the reader that the latter proposition is stated in the language of $\infty$-categories, which would be the best language suited to the formulation of the results of this section.
elements.sent-2411-960: However, we will stick with our choice, made (and justified) earlier in the paper, of avoiding the language of $\infty$-categories in this work, even if at times this somewhat hinders the clarity of the results.
elements.sent-2411-961: In our language, for the sober topological space $X = \SpecNC(A)$ with $A \in \bH \bRings_\Z$ and given homotopical epimorphism $A \to B_U$, \cite[Proposition 10.5]{Scholze provides sufficient conditions under which the association \begin{equation}\SpecNC(B_U)= U \mapsto \bH \bMod_{B_U}\,,\end{equation} is a homotopical sheaf of pre-triangulated categories.}
elements.sent-2411-962: This implies, for example, that the association \begin{equation}U \mapsto B_U\end{equation} is a homotopical sheaf of rings, which means, in turn, that given a cover $\{ A \to B_{U_i \}_{i \in I}$ for the homotopical Zariski topology we have \begin{equation}A \cong \R \lim N^\bullet(B_{U_i})\,.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-963: Here $N^\bullet(B_{U_i )$ denotes the \u{C ech nerve of the cover, often referred to as cobar construction, given by the cosimplicial object \begin{equation}\prod_{i \in I} B_{U_i} \rightrightarrows \prod_{i, j \in I} B_{U_i} \otimes_{A}^\L B_{U_j} \mathrel{\substack{\textstyle\rightarrow [-0.6ex] \textstyle\rightarrow [-0.6ex] \textstyle\rightarrow}}\cdots.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-199: \begin{equation}\prod_{i \in I} B_{U_i} \rightrightarrows \prod_{i, j \in I} B_{U_i} \otimes_{A}^\L B_{U_j} \mathrel{\substack{\textstyle\rightarrow [-0.6ex] \textstyle\rightarrow [-0.6ex] \textstyle\rightarrow}}\cdots.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-964: Note that condition (i) in \cite[Proposition 10.5]{Scholze essentially never holds unless $A$ is either a commutative ring or a cdga.}
elements.sent-2411-965: And as we have seen, one can check that the above associations do not give homotopical sheaves.
elements.sent-2411-966: But \cite[Proposition 10.5]{Scholze has another interpretation in terms of comonadic descent.}
elements.sent-2411-967: Indeed, its statement implies that, under the above hypothesis, the adjunction \begin{equation}\label{eq:comonadic_adjunction} \bH \bMod_A \leftrightarrows \bigoplus_{i \in I} \bH \bMod_{B_{U_i}}\end{equation} given by base change and restriction of scalars functors, is homotopically comonadic.
elements.sent-2411-968: The additional piece of information given by (the proof of) \cite[Proposition 10.5]{Scholze is that under the proposition's hypothesis, the descent data is given by a finite diagram\footnote{The theory of $\infty$-categories makes this statement very clear.}}
elements.sent-2411-969: We do not comment further here.. .
elements.sent-2411-970: Let us now show that in our situation we still have homotopical descent, but the descent data is no longer contained in a finite diagram, and the resulting cobar construction of modules and algebras over $A$ are not equal to the restriction of section of sheaves on the topological space $X$ as in the commutative case.
elements.sent-2411-971: It is always possible to reconstruct a module $M \in \bH \bMod_A$ uniquely from its localizations $M \otimes_A B_{U_i}$, and, furthermore, any descent data on the cover $\{ A \to B_{U_i \}_{i \in I}$, formally given by a comodule over the comonad associated to the adjunction \eqref{eq:comonadic_adjunction , comes from a module over $A$.}}
elements.sent-2411-972: Thus, our goal is to prove that the adjunction \eqref{eq:comonadic_adjunction is comonadic.}
elements.sent-2411-973: To do this we introduce the following generalisation (and adaptation) of the terminology introduced in \cite{Mathew in the commutative setting.}
elements.sent-2411-974: We say that $B$ admits descent or is descendable if the smallest smashing subcategory of $\bH \bMod_A$ containing $B$ is the whole $\bH \bMod_A$.
elements.sent-2411-975: Indeed, by Proposition~\ref{prop:faithful_covers we have that the family of functors $\{ \bH \bMod_A \to \bH \bMod_{B_i \}$ is faithful and in particular conservative, which implies that the smallest smashing subcategory containing $\bH \bMod_{B_i}$ in $\bH \bMod_A$ --- and hence the one generated by $\prod_{i \in I B_i$ --- is the whole $\bH \bMod_A$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-976: We will follow closely the discussion from \cite[Section 3]{Mathew .}

elements.sent-2411-980: The next theorem shows how comonadic descent is related to homotopy limits.

elements.sent-2411-1117: A closer examination reveals that the latter is not a suitable notion for the noncommutative setting, in that it does not satisfy the axioms of a Grothendieck topology.
elements.sent-2411-1118: As it turns out, one can circumvent this issue by working directly in the category $\bH\bRings_\Z$ and requiring conservativity for the base change functors at the level of algebras.
elements.sent-2411-1119: The price one has to pay is that the notion of coverage one obtains is harder to handle in many respects and, furthermore, a direct comparison with the classical Zariski topology is precluded.

elements.section-2411-008: Towards a noncommutative notion of sheaf
elements.sent-2411-1310: However, the noncommutative situation is more complicated for a number of reasons.
elements.sent-2411-1311: For starters, we have considered two spectra so far: $\SpecNC(A)$ and $\SpecNC_\fine(A)$.
elements.sent-2411-1312: Finally --- and most crucially --- one cannot directly use the theory of (homotopical) comonadic descent as in (derived) algebraic geometry.
elements.sent-2411-1313: We shall now perform an examination of these issues in some detail.
elements.sent-2411-1314: In this subsection we would like start investigating applications of sheaf theory to $\SpecNC(A)$ and $\SpecNC_\fine(A)$.
elements.sent-2411-1315: The main issue we will face is that we can provide an example of noncommutative ring where the natural structure pre-sheaf is a sheaf on $\SpecNC(A)$ but its pullback (as a pre-sheaf) to $\SpecNC_\fine(A)$ is not a sheaf.
elements.sent-2411-1316: Furthermore, its sheafification does not satisfy the properties a structure sheaf on $\SpecNC_\fine(A)$ should possess.
elements.sent-2411-1317: This motives us to generalise the notion of sheaf using the theory of comonadic descent.
elements.sent-2411-1318: In subsection \ref{sec:noncommutative_spectra we have seen that $X = \SpecNC_\fine(A) = \{p_1, p_2, p_3\}$ is the discrete space with three points.}
elements.sent-2411-1319: These three points, being open subsets, correspond to three homotopical epimorphisms $A \to k$, $A \to M_2(k)$ and $A \to k$ (see Example \ref{exa:A_2_descent below for more information about how to compute these homotopical epimorphisms).}
elements.sent-2411-1320: Therefore, there is still a local-to-global principle that the pre-sheaf $\cO_X$ over $\SpecNC_\fine(A)$ and modules over it satisfy.
elements.sent-2411-1321: Note that $P_1$ and $P_2$ are projective left $A$-modules, $A \cong P_1 \oplus P_2$ as a left $A$-module, whereas $S_2$ has the left $A$-module structure induced by the quotient map $P_2 \to S_2$ and it is obviously indecomposable.
elements.sent-2411-1322: These submodules correspond to indecomposable representations of $A_2$ and to generators of the (non-trivial) smashing subcategories of ${ _A \bH \bMod$, as discussed in subsection \ref{sec:noncommutative_spectra .}}
elements.sent-2411-1323: If we now go back to the discrete topological space $X = \SpecNC_\fine(A) = \{ p_1, p_2, p_3 \}$, we would expect the intersection of any two points to be empty.
